title: Dynamic Programming II
summary: You already know 1D/2D counting DP — this module is about the **shape of the state**. Three families cover almost every "hard" OA DP: **grid DP** (walk a matrix), **two-sequence DP** (align two strings), and **interval DP** (solve a range by splitting it). Nail the state definition for each and Trilogy's hard-DP round and Flipkart's string hards collapse into templates.

notes.heading: One skill, three shapes of state
notes.body:
In DP I you learned the mechanics: memoize a recursion, or fill a table bottom-up. This module is about something subtler — the **shape of the state**. Almost every "hard" DP that shows up in OAs is one of three families, and each family has a signature you can spot in the problem statement before you write a line of code.

| You see... | Family | State |
|---|---|---|
| A grid/matrix, moves restricted (right/down, 4-neighbours), "count paths" / "cheapest path" | **Grid DP** | `dp[i][j]` = best answer *reaching* cell `(i,j)` |
| Two strings/arrays to compare, align, or edit into each other | **Two-sequence DP** | `dp[i][j]` = answer for prefixes `a[0..i-1]`, `b[0..j-1]` |
| Contiguous substrings + a range property (palindrome), or "split / merge / burst" where operation ORDER matters | **Interval DP** | `dp[i][j]` = answer for the range `[i..j]` |
| "Largest square/rectangle of 1s in a binary matrix" | **Matrix DP** | `dp[i][j]` = best shape with bottom-right corner at `(i,j)` |

> [!KEY]
> The whole game is one sentence: **define the state so that the answer at a cell depends only on already-computed cells**, then pick a loop order that respects those dependencies. Grid → row-major. Two-sequence → prefix order. Interval → by increasing length. Everything else in this module is a corollary of that sentence.

> [!EXAM]
> Calibrate your effort: Trilogy's hard-DP round **loves Maximal Square** (it's the 🎯 in this ladder — memorize it cold), and Flipkart's 3-hards-in-90-minutes format is where Edit Distance and interval DP show up. Microsoft's Codility mediums stay closer to the grid-path family.

notes.heading: Form A — grid path DP: ask each cell who stepped into it
Form B — two-sequence DP: the LCS skeleton
Form C — interval DP: solve ranges by length
notes.body:
Third shape, and the one you've genuinely never done: the answer for a **contiguous range** `[i..j]` is built from smaller ranges inside it. The signature phrases: "split the array/string optimally", "merge stones", "burst balloons" — anywhere the **order of operations** matters, so a greedy left-to-right pass can't work.

The template picks a split point `k` and combines the two halves plus the cost of the operation that joins them:

```cpp
// Generic interval DP: answer(i,j) built from smaller ranges. Iterate by LENGTH.
int intervalDP(int n) {
    vector<vector<int>> dp(n, vector<int>(n, 0));
    for (int len = 2; len <= n; len++)          // grow interval length
        for (int i = 0; i + len - 1 < n; i++) { // left endpoint
            int j = i + len - 1;                 // right endpoint
            dp[i][j] = INT_MAX;                  // or 0, depends on problem
            for (int k = i; k < j; k++)          // split point / last op
                dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j],
                               dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + cost(i, k, j));
        }
    return dp[0][n-1];
}
```

[[fig:m13-interval-order]]

> [!KEY]
> The **outer loop is the interval length**. A range of length `len` only ever depends on strictly shorter ranges, so solving all length-2 ranges, then all length-3, and so on guarantees `dp[i][k]` and `dp[k+1][j]` are ready when you read them. Length is the "time axis" of interval DP.

> [!PITFALL]
> The most common interval-DP mistake: writing a plain row-major `for i, for j` like grid DP. Then `dp[k+1][j]` lives in a **later row** you haven't touched yet — you read zeros/garbage and get a wrong answer with no crash to warn you. If your interval DP returns something suspiciously small, check the loop order first.

> [!INTUITION]
> When the split-point recurrence doesn't obviously work (Burst Balloons!), flip the question: instead of "what operation happens **first**?", ask "what operation happens **last** in this range?". The last operation sees a fixed, known environment, which is exactly what decouples the two halves. That reframe is the deep-dive below, and it's the reusable trick behind Minimum Cost to Cut a Stick and Matrix Chain Multiplication.

notes.heading: The palindrome table: interval DP's little sibling
Pitfalls, space compression, and how to narrate DP
notes.body:
The recurrences above are the easy 80%. Here's the 20% that actually costs points in OAs, plus the script for talking about DP in interviews.

**The classic bugs, ranked by how often they bite:**

- **Base rows/columns forgotten.** Edit distance needs `dp[i][0] = i` and `dp[0][j] = j` *before* the double loop. Grid counting needs the first row/column seeded to 1 — and to 0 past an obstacle. A wrong base case doesn't crash; it silently corrupts everything downstream.
- **Prefix off-by-one.** `(m+1) × (n+1)` table, compare `a[i-1]` with `b[j-1]`. Every time.
- **Interval order.** Outer loop = length (or `i` descending / `j` ascending). Never plain row-major.
- **Maximal Square returns the area**, not the side. `return side * side;` — people lose a full test case on this exact line.

> [!WARNING]
> **Space compression has one trap.** Grid and two-sequence DP only read the previous row, so a rolling 1D array gives $O(n)$ space — but LCS and edit distance also read the **diagonal** `dp[i-1][j-1]`, which the rolling row overwrites. Stash it in a temp (`int prev = dp[j-1]` *before* updating) or you'll compute nonsense. If the follow-up comes, mention the temp — that detail is what the interviewer is fishing for.

> [!KEY] The five-step narration
> Say it in this order, out loud, before coding: (1) "Let me define the state — `dp[i][j]` means…" (2) "The recurrence: I get here from a smaller subproblem by…" (3) "Base cases are…" (4) "Iteration order, so dependencies are ready, is…" (5) "Complexity is…". Stating the state *first* signals seniority — and it physically stops you from coding a wrong recurrence, because a wrong state won't survive step 2.

> [!EXAM]
> Under time pressure, **always write the $O(mn)$ 2D table first and get it correct**. Only compress to $O(n)$ if there's time left over. A correct 2D solution scores full marks; a buggy 1D one scores zero. This trade is never close.

problems.name: Unique Paths
problems.note: Grid **counting** DP: `dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]`; seed the top row and left column with 1.

problems.name: Unique Paths II
problems.note: Same recurrence, but an obstacle forces `dp = 0` — and watch the edge case where the **start cell** itself is blocked.

problems.name: Minimum Path Sum
problems.note: `min` instead of count — the Form A template verbatim.

problems.name: Triangle
problems.note: Grid DP on a jagged triangle; **bottom-up** is cleanest — each cell takes `min` of its two children.

problems.name: Maximal Square
problems.note: The **Trilogy STAR**: `dp = 1 + min(up, left, diagonal)`; return `side * side`, not the side. Memorize this.

problems.name: Longest Common Subsequence
problems.note: The parent two-sequence recurrence: match → `1 + diagonal`, else `max(up, left)`.

problems.name: Edit Distance
problems.note: Insert/delete/replace = right/up/diagonal; base rows `dp[i][0]=i`, `dp[0][j]=j`. A classic Microsoft/Flipkart favourite.

problems.name: Delete Operation for Two Strings
problems.note: LCS in disguise: answer = `m + n - 2*LCS(a, b)`.

problems.name: Longest Palindromic Substring
problems.note: Interval-style boolean `dp[i][j]`; expand-around-center also works ($O(n^2)$ time, $O(1)$ space).

problems.name: Palindromic Substrings
problems.note: Count instead of longest — the same boolean `dp[i][j]` table, `cnt++` on every true cell.

problems.name: Longest Palindromic Subsequence
problems.note: Interval DP on `[i..j]` — or the shortcut: LCS of `s` and `reverse(s)`.

problems.name: Maximal Rectangle
problems.note: Matrix DP + histogram/monotonic stack per row; the tougher sibling of Maximal Square.

problems.name: Burst Balloons
problems.note: The canonical **last-operation** interval DP: pad with 1s, pick the balloon popped LAST in `(i, j)`. Genuinely hard, huge payoff.

problems.name: Minimum Cost to Cut a Stick
problems.note: Interval DP over **cut positions** — the same skeleton as Burst Balloons, applied to sorted cuts.

deepdives.q: **Maximal Square** — given a binary matrix, find the largest square containing only 1s and return its **area**. Why does the recurrence take the *min* of three neighbours?
**Edit Distance** — minimum number of insert / delete / replace operations to turn string `a` into string `b`. Where do the three options in the recurrence come from, and what are the base cases?
deepdives.answer:
Let `dp[i][j]` = edit distance between the prefixes `a[0..i-1]` and `b[0..j-1]`, on the usual `(m+1) × (n+1)` padded table. Stare at the **last characters** of the two prefixes:

- If `a[i-1] == b[j-1]`: they pair up for free — the problem reduces to `dp[i-1][j-1]`.
- Otherwise you must perform exactly one of three operations, then solve what remains:
  - **replace** `a[i-1]` with `b[j-1]`: `1 + dp[i-1][j-1]`
  - **delete** `a[i-1]`: `1 + dp[i-1][j]`
  - **insert** `b[j-1]` at the end of `a`: `1 + dp[i][j-1]`

```cpp
if (a[i-1] == b[j-1]) dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1];
else dp[i][j] = 1 + min({ dp[i-1][j-1],   // replace
                          dp[i-1][j],     // delete from a
                          dp[i][j-1] });  // insert into a
```

> [!KEY]
> Each of the three edits maps to exactly one **direction in the table**: diagonal = match-or-replace, up = delete, left = insert. The table is an *alignment grid*, and edit distance is the cheapest monotone path through it — the same grid a `diff` tool walks. Once you hold that picture, you can re-derive the recurrence in ten seconds instead of memorizing it.

> [!PITFALL]
> **The base cases are the trap**, not the recurrence: `dp[i][0] = i` (turning a length-`i` prefix into the empty string costs `i` deletions) and `dp[0][j] = j` (building `b`'s prefix from nothing costs `j` insertions). Fill both *before* the double loop. Forget them and every cell downstream inherits the corruption — with no crash to tip you off.

Fill row-major like LCS; the answer is `dp[m][n]`.

**Complexity:** time $O(mn)$, space $O(mn)$ → $O(n)$ with a rolling row **plus a temp for the diagonal** (it gets overwritten before you'd read it).

**What generalizes:** the "decide the fate of the last character, reduce to a smaller prefix pair" move powers One Edit Distance, Delete Operation for Two Strings, and regex/wildcard matching — same table, different transition menu.

deepdives.q: **Palindromic Substrings** — count how many substrings of `s` are palindromes. How do you beat the $O(n^3)$ brute force, and why is the iteration order `i` **down**, `j` **up**?

**Burst Balloons** — bursting balloon `i` earns `nums[i-1] * nums[i] * nums[i+1]` with neighbours evaluated *at burst time*; maximize total coins. Why does the natural "burst first" DP fail, and what fixes it?
deepdives.answer:
**Why the obvious DP fails.** Try "which balloon do I burst *first* in this range?" — bursting it splits the array into two halves, but the halves **aren't independent**: after the left half loses balloons, the right half's boundary neighbour changes, so `dp` on raw halves is meaningless. This is the rare problem where the natural recursion is simply wrong, not just slow.

> [!KEY] Reverse the question
> Ask instead: **"which balloon `k` is burst LAST in the open interval `(i, j)`?"** If `k` is last, then at the moment it pops, everything strictly between `i` and `j` is already gone — so its live neighbours are exactly the *boundaries* `nums[i]` and `nums[j]`, which never change. Fixing the last operation is what decouples the two sides.

[[fig:m13-burst-last]]

Pad the array with `1` at both ends. Define `dp[i][j]` = max coins from bursting **everything strictly between** `i` and `j` (exclusive boundaries):

```cpp
// dp[i][j] = max coins bursting all balloons strictly between i and j (exclusive ends)
for (int len = 2; len <= n - 1; len++)          // distance between boundaries
    for (int i = 0; i + len <= n - 1; i++) {
        int j = i + len;
        for (int k = i + 1; k < j; k++)          // k = last balloon in (i,j)
            dp[i][j] = max(dp[i][j],
                dp[i][k] + dp[k][j]              // sub-intervals, already solved
                + nums[i] * nums[k] * nums[j]);  // k bursts against fixed i, j
    }
// answer: dp[0][n-1] over the padded array
```

Because `k` dies last, everything in `(i, k)` is burst while `k` is still alive and acting as a wall, and likewise for `(k, j)` — so `dp[i][k]` and `dp[k][j]` are fully independent subproblems. That independence is the *entire* reason interval DP works here.

**Complexity:** $O(n^2)$ intervals × $O(n)$ choices of `k` = time $O(n^3)$, space $O(n^2)$.

> [!EXAM]
> The "condition on the LAST operation" reframe is directly reusable: **Minimum Cost to Cut a Stick** (which cut do I make first on `[i..j]`? — equivalently, condition on the segment boundaries) and **Matrix Chain Multiplication** (which multiplication happens last?) are the same skeleton. If a Flipkart hard says "burst/merge/split with order-dependent cost", type this template.

quiz.q: An OA problem reads: "Stone piles sit in a row; you repeatedly merge two *adjacent* piles, paying their combined weight; minimize the total cost of merging everything." Which DP family fits, and what is the state?
options: Grid path DP — `dp[i][j]` = cheapest way to reach pile `(i, j)`
Two-sequence DP — `dp[i][j]` over prefixes of the pile array and its reverse
Interval DP — `dp[i][j]` = min cost to merge the contiguous range `[i..j]`, split at `k`, iterate by length
0/1 knapsack — `dp[w]` = min cost using piles of total weight `w`
quiz.explanation: Merges are on *contiguous* ranges and the ORDER of merges changes the cost — the two signatures of interval DP. The final merge of `[i..j]` joins some `[i..k]` and `[k+1..j]`, giving `dp[i][j] = min over k of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + sum(i..j)`. Grid DP needs a lattice walk, two-sequence DP needs two strings to align, and knapsack needs independent take/skip items — none of which describe adjacent merging.

quiz.q: You take the interval DP template but replace the outer `for (len = 2; ...)` loop with a plain row-major scan: `for (i = 0..n-1) for (j = i+1..n-1)`. What actually happens?
options: `dp[k+1][j]` is read from rows below `i` that haven't been computed yet, so you silently combine garbage/zero values into a wrong answer
Nothing — row-major order still computes every dependency before it's needed
The program crashes with an out-of-bounds access on `dp[k+1][j]`
It's still correct but the complexity degrades from O(n²) to O(n³)
quiz.explanation: In row-major order, `dp[i][k]` is fine (same row, earlier `j`), but `dp[k+1][j]` with `k+1 > i` lives in a LATER row — still holding its initial value. The indices are all in range, so nothing crashes: you just get a quietly wrong (usually too-small) answer. That silence is exactly why loop order is the #1 interval-DP bug. Iterating by length guarantees both halves are strictly shorter and therefore done.

quiz.q: Burst Balloons on an array of `n` balloons (after padding with 1s at both ends): what is the time complexity of the interval DP solution?
options: $O(n^2 \log n)$
quiz.explanation: There are $O(n^2)$ intervals `(i, j)`, and each one tries every possible last balloon `k` strictly inside it — $O(n)$ choices. $O(n^2) \times O(n) = O(n^3)$. $O(n^2)$ forgets the inner split loop; $O(2^n)$ is the memoryless brute force the DP exists to kill.

quiz.q: You run the LCS template on `a = "ab"`, `b = "ba"`. What is `dp[2][2]` (the final answer)?
options: 2, but only if you iterate column-major instead of row-major
quiz.explanation: The common *subsequences* of "ab" and "ba" are "a" and "b" — you can't have both, because they appear in opposite orders. So LCS = 1. Trace: dp[1][1] (a vs b) = 0, dp[1][2] (a vs ba) = 1, dp[2][1] (ab vs b) = 1, dp[2][2]: 'b' != 'a' so max(dp[1][2], dp[2][1]) = 1. Iteration order between row- and column-major never changes the values — dependencies are ready either way.

quiz.q: In the palindrome table condition `s[i] == s[j] && (j - i < 2 || dp[i+1][j-1])`, what is the `j - i < 2` check really doing?
options: It's a micro-optimization; removing it keeps the code correct but slower
It restricts the table to even-length palindromes, which need special handling
It declares length-1 and length-2 substrings palindromes without reading `dp[i+1][j-1]`, which for them is an inverted/invalid inner range
It prevents integer overflow when `i` and `j` are both large
quiz.explanation: For `j == i` (one char) and `j - i == 1` (two equal adjacent chars) there is no "inside" to verify — and `dp[i+1][j-1]` would have `i+1 > j-1`, an inverted range holding meaningless (or out-of-range) data. The short-circuit `||` must fire first. It's correctness, not speed — so option A is the tempting trap.

quiz.q: In edit distance, the base case is `dp[i][0] = i`. What does this encode?
options: Turning the first `i` characters of `a` into the empty string costs exactly `i` deletions
Inserting `i` characters of `b` into an empty `a`
Replacing the first `i` characters of `a` in place
It's an arbitrary initialization; any non-negative value works
quiz.explanation: `dp[i][0]` compares a length-`i` prefix of `a` against the EMPTY prefix of `b`: the only way to get there is to delete all `i` characters, one operation each. (Its mirror, `dp[0][j] = j`, is the insertion story — option B describes that cell, not this one.) These bases are not decorative: every interior cell ultimately chains back to them, so a wrong base corrupts the whole table.

quiz.q: Filling the Maximal Square table you reach a cell where `matrix[i][j] == '1'`, with neighbour values up = 3, left = 1, diagonal = 2. What is `dp[i][j]`?
quiz.explanation: `dp[i][j] = 1 + min(3, 1, 2) = 1 + 1 = 2`. The left neighbour only supports a side-1 square, so however generous up and the diagonal are, the region to the left has a hole beyond side 1 — you can only guarantee a 2×2 ending here. Answer 4 is the `max` mistake; answer 3 averages; answer 1 forgets the `+1` for the current cell.

quiz.q: You compress LCS from a 2D table to one rolling 1D row to get $O(n)$ space. What extra care does the match case `1 + dp[i-1][j-1]` demand?
options: None — the rolling row already holds both the up and left values you need
You must iterate `j` from right to left instead of left to right
Space compression is impossible for LCS; you need at least two full rows forever
Save the old `dp[j-1]` (last row's diagonal) into a temp *before* overwriting it, because the rolling update destroys it
quiz.explanation: In a rolling row, by the time you compute position `j`, slot `j-1` has already been overwritten with the CURRENT row's value — but the match case needs last row's `dp[i-1][j-1]`. Fix: keep `prev` holding the pre-overwrite value as you sweep. Option B is the trick for a different family (unbounded vs 0/1 knapsack); option C is false — one row plus one temp suffices. This exact temp is what interviewers listen for on the "can you do O(n) space?" follow-up.

m13-square-min3.caption: **Maximal Square.** Left: the largest all-1 square (side 3, outlined). Right: the dp table — the bottom-right corner of that square holds `1 + min(up, left, diagonal) = 1 + min(2,2,2) = 3`. The min is the weakest supporting corner.

m13-interval-order.caption: **Interval DP iteration order.** Solve every length-2 range first, then length-3, then length-4: each pass only reads answers from strictly shorter ranges, so every dependency is ready. Plain row-major order breaks this guarantee.
m13-interval-order.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 340" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Interval DP fill order: all length-2 ranges, then length-3, then length-4"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">interval DP fill order: shorter ranges first</text><rect x="20" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="42" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-muted">0</text><rect x="66" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="88" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-muted">1</text><rect x="112" y="44" width="44" height="44" m13-burst-last.caption: **Burst Balloons' key reframe** on padded `[1,3,1,5,8,1]`. Choose `k` as the balloon burst *last* in `(i, j)`: when it pops, only the walls remain beside it, so `dp[i][k]` and `dp[k][j]` are fully independent.
m13-burst-last.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 250" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Burst Balloons: the last balloon k popped between fixed walls i and j splits the interval into two independent halves"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">pick k = the LAST balloon popped in (i, j)</text><rect x="150" y="48" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel2 s-muted"/><text x="172" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-muted">1</text><rect x="212" y="48" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="234" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">3</text><rect x="274" y="48" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="296" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">1</text><rect x="336" y="48" width="44" height="44" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="358" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">5</text><rect x="398" y="48" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="420" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">8</text><rect x="460" y="48" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel2 s-muted"/><text x="482" y="76" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-muted">1</text><text x="172" y="116" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">i (wall)</text><text x="358" y="116" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">k (last)</text><text x="482" y="116" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">j (wall)</text><line x1="212" y1="146" x2="318" y2="146" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="212" y1="140" x2="212" y2="152" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="318" y1="140" x2="318" y2="152" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2"/><text x="265" y="174" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok">dp[i][k]</text><line x1="398" y1="146" x2="442" y2="146" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="398" y1="140" x2="398" y2="152" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="442" y1="140" x2="442" y2="152" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2"/><text x="420" y="174" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok">dp[k][j]</text><text x="20" y="210" class="t-sm">k pops last, so its live neighbours are exactly the walls: coins = nums[i] * nums[k] * nums[j]</text><text x="20" y="234" class="t-sm t-muted">the two halves can never touch each other — they are independent subproblems</text></svg>
